## [00:00 - 03:04]

Eye contact. Let's talk about eye contact when it's one on one, first of all. When you're speaking to someone one on one, you generally want to be looking at them most of the time. Now the rule is you want to be looking at them and finish a thought. Once you finish a thought or they've finished a thought with you, look away for a second, process the information then look back at them. Or while you're talking, talk and then look away for a second and then look back at them. It's totally fine to break eye contact. It's absolutely fine. And there are many natural reasons for you to break eye contact. For example, if you were talking about, oh, when I was typing on my computer, you can look away for a second and focus on your hand gestures while you're doing the typing. That's a natural reason for you to break eye contact is again, hand gestures. Another natural reason is when you're thinking and processing. So if someone's talking to you and they're speaking and you're listening, you can go, huh, that's really interesting. So you can break eye contact to show that you're processing for a moment. These are the different reasons where you can break eye contact naturally. Now, when you're looking at the person one on one, generally you want to look at them while you're speaking until you've finished a point. And then you can give yourself permission to look away for a moment and then continue talking. And I know this is very trite and most of you probably know this already and this is common knowledge. But I just want to make sure I share these thoughts in case you you're not very good at this like me when I first started. So for reference, that's generally about five seconds, you want to be looking at the person and then you break away for a moment and then you look back. Of course, when you're starting out and you're trying to get better at this, this is something that you just want to keep as a reference in your head. Don't literally count five seconds in your head, then look away, then look back. Don't do that routinely. You just want to use that as a reference. When I was improving my eye contact, there was a little technique that my teachers taught me, because I suffered a lot from eye contact simply because I had very low levels of self-esteem. I wasn't very confident. So to look at someone for extended periods of time for me was very daunting, especially when I spoke to the opposite sex, for example. So they taught me the triangle method. And the triangle method is you look at the left eye, then you look at the right eye, then you look at their nose, then you look at the left eye, then you look at the right eye, and then you look at their nose. So I've got a little smiley face drawn on my monitor here. So see if you can tell if I'm changing from eye to eye, then to nose. So if I look at your left eye, then I look at your right eye, then I look at your nose. Left eye, right eye, nose. Probably doesn't look any different to you at all. So this is a great technique to just take the pressure off, especially if you're finding eye contact difficult. Another way that I use to get better eye contact is I started to increase the amount of eye contact I gave people that I felt comfortable with. So my family, my friends, some of my colleagues, and then they, my teachers taught me this game to play and this helped me immensely. But when I was walking around in the city or in a public setting, I try to catch people's eyes. So I'd look at people and the moment they looked at me, then I'd smile and then I'd look to somebody else and when they look at me and I'd

## [06:05 - 08:17]

The next thing you should do is give random coverage. You wanna give good coverage, but random coverage. Be random, so you speak to this person, then move over to this side of the room and speak to that person, oh, I haven't forgot you in the middle, and then back to this side of the room, and then back to the middle, and then, oh, of course, I'll speak to you too. And then to the back of the room, to the front of the room, to the back of the room again, to the left side, so keep it random. And that is because the moment you do sequential, people get distracted from the message. And that's it. I told you the list of things to do when it comes to eye contact was short. All you've gotta do is finish a thought, make sure it's random, and give good coverage so that most of the room fills the love. This is just like when you use singular language and you start to use the word, it's good to see you here today. Instead of it's good to see you all here today. When you use singular language, it helps people feel more connected to you. It feels like you're talking to them. When you say you, instead of you guys, you folks, you say you, that's singular language used in a way to connect with every single audience member. That makes them feel when they walk away like, wow, I feel like Vin was talking to me. Eye contact is the same thing. You wanna give eye contact to this person, then move over there, look at that person. This allows people to go, wow, it felt like Vin was talking to me. It felt like he was looking at me. Because again, when you're speaking on stage, when you just look to this side of the room, you hit about 10, 20, 30 people depending on how big the stage is. It just looks like you're looking at them. That's why you wanna give good coverage. Good coverage paired with the use of singular language. It hates good to see you. People just go, wow, I feel like you're talking to me. And the final thing I'll say here as a last quick tip is look for the smiling faces in the room. Avoid the people who are grumpy and in the audience and just don't wanna be there. There's always people like that in the room. And where you can, just avoid looking at them if you can, especially if you're speaking in an audience with hundreds of people. Don't look immediately to the people who look upset. I used to be drawn to those people. But now when I speak to a large audience, I'm always looking for the friendly faces because it helps calm my nerves and it allows me to be the best version of me on stage.
